tldr: The tape is bifurcated: beaten-down enterprise software (NOW, WDAY, VEEV, FDS) is ripping on no catalyst while memory/analog semis (WDC, STX, SNDK, ON, KEYS, MPWR) are getting hit hard — ON's Synaptics acquisition is the only named catalyst in the group, and the rest of the semi weakness is unexplained, which is itself the signal. Deal flow is heavy and closing-oriented (SEM, AES, NFBK votes passed; VTOL, UUUU, FUL new acquisitions), suggesting a late-cycle M&A wave as rates normalize.

sections.heading: Software Rip vs. Semi Wipeout — The Great Rotation
sections.synthesis: The overnight tape is a clean barbell: left-for-dead enterprise software is being bought aggressively while red-hot semis are being sold — and critically, neither side has a catalyst on file, meaning this is pure positioning/flow, not fundamentals.

bullets.fact: NOW +9.9%, WDAY +9.2%, VEEV +8.4%, FDS +11.0% — all large/mid-cap enterprise software, all down 20-54% from 52w highs, all up on NO catalyst on file. DDOG +8.5% additionally got a Truist Hold→Buy upgrade with PT 300 (+25% upside).
bullets.read: This is a classic mean-reversion squeeze in names that were left for dead. NOW and WDAY at fwdP/E 20 and 10 respectively with 50%+ drawdowns suggest the market had priced in structural impairment; the no-catalyst rally says shorts are covering or allocators are rebalancing out of crowded semi winners into neglected software. The DDOG upgrade (fwdP/E 84, only -14% from highs, YTD +76%) is the odd one out — it's not beaten down, so the buy rating there is a growth-momentum call, not a value call. Watch whether NOW/WDAY follow-through holds on volume tomorrow — if it fades, this was a one-day short squeeze; if it holds, it's a durable rotation.

bullets.fact: WDC -13.2%, STX -12.2%, SNDK -10.5%, KEYS -8.7%, MPWR -8.7% — all on NO catalyst on file. ON -23.7% is the only one with a named catalyst (Synaptics acquisition). SNDK options show $151.5M two-way flow with biggest call at $1500 3dte.
bullets.read: The memory trio (WDC, STX, SNDK) all down double-digits with no catalyst is the most important signal on the tape. These are mega-caps (SNDK $310B, WDC/STX ~$200B) that ran 227-781% YTD — this smells like a crowded-long unwind or a leak ahead of a negative data point (possibly AI capex concerns or inventory build). SNDK's $1500 3dte call is deep OTM and likely a hedge against long stock, not a directional bullish bet. ON's -23.7% on the Synaptics deal is a classic acquirer penalty — the market hates the dilution/execution risk — but the magnitude suggests the deal terms (still undisclosed in the 425) may be worse than expected. Watch the S-4 for price/structure; if it's stock-heavy, ON could have further downside. KEYS and MPWR selling off alongside suggests the semi weakness is broad-based, not deal-specific.

bullets.fact: MRNA +12.6% on no catalyst, YTD +128%, fwdP/E negative (still loss-making), only -2.9% from 52w high.
bullets.read: MRNA's move is notable because it's near highs and already up 128% YTD — this isn't a beaten-down bounce like the software names. With no catalyst on file and a negative forward P/E, this is either a sentiment/positioning move tied to broader healthcare rotation (VEEV also +8.4%) or a leak ahead of pipeline news. The fact that it's near 52w highs means this is momentum extension, not recovery — higher risk of reversal if no catalyst materializes. Watch for any pipeline/contract news today.

sections.heading: Deal Flow — Votes Clearing, New Acquisitions Launching
sections.synthesis: The filing tape shows a two-track M&A environment: previously announced deals are clearing their last shareholder/regulatory hurdles (SEM, AES, NFBK, NFG), while new transformative acquisitions are being launched (ON-SYNA, UUUU-VAC, FUL-AMS, VTOL-Berry) — consistent with a late-cycle environment where companies use M&A to plug growth gaps.

bullets.fact: SEM stockholders approved take-private by Ortenzio/Jackson/WCAS consortium (79.88% of outstanding voted yes). AES stockholders approved $15.00/sh GIP-EQT buyout (97.92% of votes cast in favor). NFBK shareholders approved Columbia Financial merger. NFG's PUCO approved $2.62B Vectren Ohio acquisition.
bullets.read: Four deals clearing key conditions in one overnight cycle is a high hit-rate and signals that the 2025-2026 M&A pipeline is entering its closing phase. SEM and AES are take-privates — their approval removes the main binary risk, so spread compression should accelerate; the remaining risk is regulatory, which for AES (BlackRock/GIP/EQT infrastructure) could still be non-trivial given antitrust scrutiny of large asset-manager infrastructure deals. NFG's PUCO approval is the most operationally significant — $2.62B is ~35% of market cap, and regulatory approval was the highest-risk condition. These are all now 'watch for close' situations.

bullets.fact: ON Semiconductor announced proposed business combination with Synaptics via 425 filing; no deal terms disclosed. ON stock -23.7%. SYNA 425 filed confirming the deal.
bullets.read: The -23.7% reaction in ON is the market's verdict on deal quality before terms are even public — that's a strong negative signal. The 425 filing came via a social media post by ON's CMO, which is an unusual disclosure channel and may have contributed to the disorderly price action. The key unknown is structure: if this is a cash deal, ON's balance sheet capacity is the question; if stock, the dilution at a depressed share price is punishing. SYNA holders should watch the S-4 for the exchange ratio — the market is pricing ON as the loser here, which implies SYNA may have negotiated a strong premium. The bear case for ON: management is buying growth at the worst possible time (stock already down 32.8% from highs pre-deal). The bull case: diversifying away from pure-play analog into touch/display IoT could re-rate the business long-term.

bullets.fact: UUUU (Energy Fuels) files definitive merger agreement to acquire Vacuumschmelze for ~$718M cash plus shares — transformative move into permanent magnet manufacturing. FUL (H.B. Fuller) secures Goldman-led bridge facility for Advanced Medical Solutions acquisition. VTOL (Bristow) to acquire Berry Aviation for $105M all-cash, exit Norway offshore.
bullets.read: Three new deals, three different strategic logics. UUUU's Vacuumschmelze acquisition is the most strategically ambitious — it vertically integrates rare earths into permanent magnets, but the $718M cash + share consideration is large relative to UUUU's profile and requires significant capital raising; watch for a follow-on equity offering. FUL's bridge facility for AMS is a medical-adhesives expansion that will sit on top of $1.96B existing net debt — leverage is the key risk, and the bridge-to-permanent financing path will determine whether this is accretive or balance-sheet-stretching. VTOL's Berry Aviation deal is the cleanest: $105M cash for ~$108M revenue with 72% U.S. government exposure — that's a near 1x sales acquisition funded from cash on hand, simultaneously exiting Norway offshore to de-risk. VTOL's deal looks like the highest quality of the three.

sections.heading: Options Flow & Positioning — Defensive in Semis, Constructive in Mega-Cap Tech
sections.synthesis: Options flow confirms the price action: heavy put activity in NVDA and two-way hedging in MU/SNDK after selloffs, while MSFT/AAPL/AMD see call-heavy positioning — the market is buying protection where it's weak and expressing upside where it's strong.

bullets.fact: NVDA put-heavy: $27.8M total ($20.7M puts vs $7.1M calls), biggest put $192.5 0dte, stock -1.6%. MU two-way: $285.8M total ($144.3M calls / $141.6M puts), biggest call $1200 3dte, stock -6.7%. SNDK two-way: $151.5M total, biggest call $1500 3dte, stock -10.5%.
bullets.read: NVDA's put-heavy flow at $192.5 0dte is a near-term hedge — not a large notional but directionally consistent with the broader semi weakness. The fact that NVDA is only down 1.6% while peers are down 8-13% suggests NVDA is being used as a proxy hedge for the whole complex. MU's $285.8M two-way flow is the largest in the tape and the near-even call/put split with a $1200 3dte call (deep OTM) suggests institutional hedging of long stock positions rather than new directional bets — this is what you see when longs are nervous but not yet capitulating. SNDK's $1500 3dte call is similarly deep OTM and likely a hedge. The pattern across MU/SNDK is: longs buying protection, not bears pressing.

bullets.fact: MSFT call-heavy: $36.2M ($32.1M calls / $4.1M puts), biggest call $370 0dte, stock +5.7%. AAPL call-heavy: $33.1M ($27.9M calls / $5.3M puts), biggest call $280 0dte, stock +3.1%. AMD call-heavy: $85.3M ($65.6M calls / $19.7M puts), biggest call $520 3dte, stock -2.1%.
bullets.read: MSFT and AAPL call-heavy flow with 0dte calls is consistent with momentum-chasing into strength — both stocks are up and the flow is confirming. AMD is the interesting one: call-heavy despite stock down 2.1%, with a $520 3dte call — this could be a contrarian bet that the semi selloff is overdone, or it could be a hedge against a short position. The Citi upgrade to Buy (PT 575, +10%) gives the AMD call flow a fundamental anchor. The contrast between AMD's bullish options flow and the rest of the semi complex's weakness is a divergence worth watching — if AMD holds while WDC/SNDK/STX continue lower, it validates the view that the memory/analog selloff is idiosyncratic, not a broad semi de-rating.

sections.heading: Analyst Changes — Semis Get Love at the Bottom, Software Mixed
sections.synthesis: Analyst actions overnight are contrarian to the price action: semis are being upgraded as they sell off (QCOM, AMD, INTC) while AAPL gets downgraded as it rallies — classic sell-side lagging the tape.

bullets.fact: QCOM upgraded UW→EW (Morgan Stanley, PT 231, +22%). AMD upgraded Neutral→Buy (Citi, PT 575, +10%). INTC upgraded UW→Buy (BofA, PT 135, +5%). DDOG upgraded Hold→Buy (Truist, PT 300, +25%). SNPS upgraded Neutral→OW (Piper, PT 550, +21%). IBM upgraded Neutral→OW (JPM, PT 291, +7%).
bullets.read: Three semi upgrades (QCOM, AMD, INTC) landing on a day when the semi complex is getting crushed is either well-timed contrarian call or sell-side catching the knife. QCOM's +22% implied upside is the most attractive risk/reward and the UW→EW move suggests Morgan Stanley sees limited downside from here. INTC's UW→Buy is the most aggressive — a double-notch upgrade with only +5% upside suggests BofA's call is more about 'downside risk has passed' than 'big upside ahead.' The IBM and SNPS upgrades are quieter but meaningful: IBM at +7% from JPM is a slow-money re-rating call, and SNPS at +21% from Piper is a growth re-acceleration bet. DDOG's upgrade aligns with its +8.5% move and confirms the software bid.

bullets.fact: HD downgraded Outperform→Peer Perform (Wolfe, no PT). AAPL downgraded Outperform→Hold (KGI, PT 315, +11%). PLD downgraded Sector Outperform→Sector Perform (Scotiabank, PT 146, +4%). PLTR upgraded Underperform→Peer Perform (Wolfe). COP upgraded Neutral→Buy (Roth, PT 130, +23%). XOM upgraded Neutral→Buy (BofA, PT 154, +13%).
bullets.read: HD's downgrade by Wolfe with no PT is a pure sentiment call — likely tied to housing/consumer macro concerns. AAPL's downgrade from KGI (a Taiwan-based shop) is notable for its timing alongside the +3.1% rally and call-heavy options flow — KGI may be calling a top on the AI-iPhone narrative. PLD's downgrade with only +4% upside is a 'take profits' call on the data-center REIT trade. The COP and XOM energy upgrades (Roth +23%, BofA +13%) are a coordinated bullish tilt on energy that may reflect geopolitical risk premium or commodity supply tightening — watch if energy stocks catch a bid today. PLTR's UW→Peer Perform from Wolfe is a 'less bearish' call, not a bullish one — the stock is still rated in-line.

sections.heading: Corporate Governance & Capital Structure Signals
sections.synthesis: The filing tape contains several governance and capital-structure moves that are individually small but collectively signal companies preparing balance sheets for either distress or opportunity — credit amendments, CFO changes, and debt issuances all point to active balance-sheet management.

bullets.fact: GT (Goodyear) CFO Zamarro departing with ~8 days notice; interim CFO named. HNRG credit agreement amended: leverage covenant raised from 2.50x to 4.25x. APLD new Goldman-led syndicated credit facility refinances existing debt. IRM prices $1.5B 6.25% senior notes due 2035. HASI prices $1.0B 5.95% green notes due 2033.
bullets.read: GT's CFO departure with 8 days notice at a highly levered company is a red flag — even with the 'no disagreement' disclosure, the speed of the exit and interim replacement suggests either an internal conflict or an external opportunity that couldn't wait. This is the kind of filing that warrants caution. HNRG's covenant relaxation from 2.50x to 4.25x is a near-doubling of leverage headroom — this is either proactive (funding an acquisition/growth) or reactive (existing leverage was approaching the cap). APLD's Goldman-led refinance is a positive signal — upgrading from bilateral loans (FNBO, Macquarie) to a syndicated facility suggests improving credit quality and lender confidence. IRM's $1.5B at 6.25% and HASI's $1.0B at 5.95% are both sizable issuance — IRM's is leverage-increasing on an already levered balance sheet, while HASI's is pipeline-funding at a locked cost. Both are rate-take calls — locking in before potential rate cuts.

bullets.fact: PAHC names Dani Bendheim CEO with 300K performance RSUs vesting only above $70 (5-year cliff). ITGR CRM & Neuromodulation President Stephens moved to interim special-projects role with March 2027 exit, no 2027 incentives. FTRE appoints new CFO Jason Knoblauch, succeeding Jill McConnell (no disagreement disclosed).
bullets.read: PAHC's CEO change with performance RSUs that only vest above $70 is a strong alignment signal — management only wins if shareholders win significantly, which suggests the board expects substantial upside or is trying to force it. ITGR's segment president being moved to a time-limited role with no 2027 incentives is a soft-exit pattern — this often precedes a segment sale or restructuring, especially given Integer's ongoing strategic review. FTRE's CFO transition is the cleanest of the three — planned succession with explicit no-disagreement language and a transition period, which is the standard 'good' pattern for CFO changes.

watchToday.text: ON-SYNA deal terms: S-4/proxy filing expected with price, structure, and synergy details — the -23.7% ON reaction means terms matter enormously for both sides
Semi complex follow-through: whether WDC/STX/SNDK/KEYS/MPWR stabilize or extend losses — no catalyst on file means any continuation is pure flow/positioning
Software rotation confirmation: whether NOW/WDAY/VEEV/FDS hold overnight gains or fade — DDOG's Truist upgrade provides a fundamental anchor for the group
CAPR FDA Advisory Committee scheduled July 29 for Deramiocel BLA in DMD — PDUFA date August 22; key binary catalyst now on the calendar
FDS reports earnings July 1 — the only name in the biggest-movers list with a near-term earnings catalyst; the +11% move into prints is either a leak or positioning
AES and SEM take-private votes cleared — watch for closing timeline updates and regulatory filings as remaining conditions
MU $285.8M options flow with 3dte calls at $1200 — largest options flow in the tape; watch for gamma-driven price action into Friday expiry
